proficiencyScale.label: Awareness
proficiencyScale.description: Knows the concept exists; can describe it at a high level

proficiencyScale.label: Foundational
proficiencyScale.description: Can execute with guidance; has applied it in limited scope

proficiencyScale.label: Practitioner
proficiencyScale.description: Works independently; applies in real environments regularly

proficiencyScale.label: Advanced
proficiencyScale.description: Deep working knowledge; can design, troubleshoot, and guide others

proficiencyScale.label: Expert
proficiencyScale.description: Authoritative; designs systems, sets standards, mentors others at this skill

skills.label: Microsoft Entra ID
masteryLevels: Can describe what Entra ID is and explain the difference between cloud-only and hybrid identity models to a colleague.
Creates users and groups, assigns RBAC roles, and configures a basic Conditional Access policy in a test tenant following documentation.
Manages Managed Identities for Azure workloads, configures SSO for SaaS applications, and enforces MFA via Conditional Access policies in production.
Designs multi-tenant identity architectures, implements Privileged Identity Management for just-in-time access, and diagnoses complex token flows and claims-mapping failures.
Defines the organisation's identity governance strategy; architects cross-tenant, B2B/B2C, and hybrid AD DS integration patterns; advises engineering teams on regulatory compliance requirements.

resources.title: Implement Microsoft Entra identity management — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure Key Vault
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure Key Vault is, describe the three object types it manages (secrets, keys, and certificates), and articulate why centralising credentials in Key Vault is preferable to application config files or environment variables.

Creates a Key Vault, stores a secret, and retrieves it from an application using the Azure SDK authenticated with a managed identity, configuring an access policy that grants the identity read access to secrets only.

Migrates application configurations from hardcoded values to Key Vault references in App Service and Azure Functions, switches from legacy access policies to RBAC-based access control, and restricts Key Vault to a private endpoint to eliminate public exposure.

Designs a Key Vault architecture for a multi-application environment with environment-separated vaults, implements automated certificate lifecycle management with auto-rotation, and configures diagnostic logging with alerts on unauthorised access attempts or policy changes.

Defines the organisation's secrets management strategy using Key Vault; establishes vault segmentation, RBAC, and key rotation policies as organisational standards; audits compliance across all subscriptions; advises on Key Vault integration patterns for Bicep IaC, CI/CD pipelines, and AKS workload identity.

resources.title: Configure and manage Azure Key Vault — MS Learn

skills.label: Azure Policy
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure Policy is, describe how it differs from RBAC (controlling what resources look like vs. what actions you can perform), and identify the key effect types: Audit, Deny, Append, Modify, and DeployIfNotExists.

Assigns a built-in policy definition to a resource group, reviews the compliance report to identify non-compliant resources, and interprets why a specific resource is flagged against the assigned definition.

Authors a custom policy definition in JSON, groups related policies into an initiative, assigns the initiative at subscription scope, and creates a remediation task to bring existing non-compliant resources into compliance using DeployIfNotExists.

Implements Policy-as-Code by managing definitions and assignments in a Git repository with CI/CD deployment, designs an initiative structure enforcing tagging standards, allowed locations, and SKU compliance across all subscriptions, and uses compliance data to produce governance posture reports.

Defines the organisation's cloud governance framework using Azure Policy; architects the initiative hierarchy across management groups; establishes the policy lifecycle from authoring through testing to enforcement mode; presents compliance posture trends to audit and security leadership.

resources.title: Build a cloud governance strategy on Azure — MS Learn

skills.label: Microsoft Defender for Cloud
masteryLevels: Can describe what Microsoft Defender for Cloud is, explain the difference between CSPM (security posture management) and CWPP (workload protection), and identify the distinction between free foundational coverage and paid Defender plans.
Enables Defender for Cloud on a subscription, reviews the Secure Score dashboard, and identifies the top recommendations that offer the greatest posture improvement for the least remediation effort.

Enables Defender plans for specific workload types (Servers, Databases, Storage), investigates and remediates security recommendations, configures regulatory compliance dashboards against a framework such as CIS or NIST SP 800-53, and sets up email alerts for high-severity threats.

Designs a Defender for Cloud deployment across a multi-subscription estate, implements auto-provisioning for monitoring agents and extensions, integrates security alerts with Microsoft Sentinel, and connects source code repositories to enable DevSecOps posture management.

Defines the organisation's cloud security posture management strategy; owns the Secure Score improvement roadmap and assigns remediation ownership to subscription teams; advises on Defender plan selection based on workload risk and cost; presents regulatory compliance posture to audit committees.

resources.title: Defender for Cloud security recommendations reference
Mitigate threats with Microsoft Defender for Cloud — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure Blueprints / Landing Zones
skills.notes: Azure Blueprints deprecated July 2026 — Landing Zone architecture via CAF is the durable skill
masteryLevels: Can describe the components of an Azure Landing Zone — management groups, subscriptions, policies, and shared platform services — and explain why a structured landing zone accelerates secure, governed cloud adoption at scale.

Deploys a basic management group hierarchy (Platform, Landing Zones, Sandboxes) and assigns built-in policies and RBAC at management group scope, demonstrating how settings inherit to all child subscriptions.

Implements a Landing Zone architecture following the Cloud Adoption Framework, configuring connectivity, identity, and management subscriptions; understands that Azure Blueprints is deprecated in favour of Bicep and Deployment Stacks for landing zone IaC delivery.

Designs and deploys a multi-subscription Landing Zone using Bicep and Deployment Stacks, configures policy inheritance to enforce compliance guardrails across all child subscriptions, and establishes a subscription vending process for self-service workload onboarding.

Defines the organisation's landing zone architecture and governance strategy; leads the platform team that owns connectivity, identity, and management subscriptions; evaluates the Azure Landing Zone accelerator; manages the platform's evolution as the organisation scales to many subscriptions.

resources.title: Azure Landing Zones documentation — Cloud Adoption Framework

skills.label: Zero Trust Architecture
masteryLevels: Can describe the Zero Trust principle of 'never trust, always verify' and contrast it with traditional perimeter-based security to a non-technical audience.
Maps an organisation's existing controls against the NIST Zero Trust Architecture pillars and produces a gap analysis identifying the highest-priority remediation actions.

Implements Conditional Access policies, enforces least-privilege access via PIM and RBAC, and configures network micro-segmentation using NSGs and Private Endpoints to eliminate implicit trust from workloads.

Designs a Zero Trust architecture spanning identity, devices, applications, network, and data planes; integrates telemetry across all planes into a SIEM for continuous validation and automated response.

Leads an organisation's Zero Trust transformation programme; evaluates vendor solutions across all pillars, defines the multi-year target-state architecture, and presents strategy and measurable progress to executive stakeholders.

skills.label: Azure Bicep
masteryLevels: Can read a Bicep file and explain what each resource declaration, parameter, and variable does without writing one independently.
Deploys a single Azure resource end-to-end by authoring a Bicep file with parameters, outputs, and a parameter file using the official documentation.
Authors multi-file Bicep deployments with reusable child modules, runs what-if before every production change, and integrates Bicep linting into a CI pipeline.

Designs and publishes a versioned module registry consumed across teams, implements deployment stacks for lifecycle-managed resource groups, and enforces tagging and policy compliance at the module layer.

Architects the organisation's entire IaC estate in Bicep; defines module versioning and governance strategy; evaluates new Bicep features to steer tooling decisions; mentors engineers on complex patterns including subscription and management-group scoped deployments.

resources.title: Fundamentals of Bicep — Microsoft Learn path

skills.label: GitHub Actions
masteryLevels: Can explain how a GitHub Actions workflow is triggered, identify jobs and steps in an existing YAML file, and describe the event-driven execution model.
Writes a workflow from a template that runs on push events, calls marketplace actions, and builds and tests a simple project without manual guidance.

Authors reusable workflows, configures OIDC-based authentication to Azure or AWS without long-lived secrets, manages environment-specific variables, and implements matrix builds for multi-platform CI.
Designs multi-stage deployment pipelines with environment protection rules, self-hosted runners, composite actions, and caching strategies that materially reduce pipeline duration.

Architects the CI/CD platform across an organisation using GitHub Actions; defines reusable workflow libraries, enforces security posture via required workflows, and optimises runner cost and performance at scale.

resources.title: Configuring OIDC in Azure — GitHub Docs

skills.label: Zero Trust Architecture
masteryLevels: Can describe the Zero Trust principle of 'never trust, always verify' and contrast it with traditional perimeter-based security to a non-technical audience.
Maps an organisation's existing controls against the NIST Zero Trust Architecture pillars and produces a gap analysis identifying the highest-priority remediation actions.

Implements Conditional Access policies, enforces least-privilege access via PIM and RBAC, and configures network micro-segmentation using NSGs and Private Endpoints to eliminate implicit trust from workloads.

Designs a Zero Trust architecture spanning identity, devices, applications, network, and data planes; integrates telemetry across all planes into a SIEM for continuous validation and automated response.

Leads an organisation's Zero Trust transformation programme; evaluates vendor solutions across all pillars, defines the multi-year target-state architecture, and presents strategy and measurable progress to executive stakeholders.

skills.label: GitHub Actions
masteryLevels: Can explain how a GitHub Actions workflow is triggered, identify jobs and steps in an existing YAML file, and describe the event-driven execution model.
Writes a workflow from a template that runs on push events, calls marketplace actions, and builds and tests a simple project without manual guidance.

Authors reusable workflows, configures OIDC-based authentication to Azure or AWS without long-lived secrets, manages environment-specific variables, and implements matrix builds for multi-platform CI.
Designs multi-stage deployment pipelines with environment protection rules, self-hosted runners, composite actions, and caching strategies that materially reduce pipeline duration.

Architects the CI/CD platform across an organisation using GitHub Actions; defines reusable workflow libraries, enforces security posture via required workflows, and optimises runner cost and performance at scale.

skills.label: GitHub Actions
masteryLevels: Can explain how a GitHub Actions workflow is triggered, identify jobs and steps in an existing YAML file, and describe the event-driven execution model.
Writes a workflow from a template that runs on push events, calls marketplace actions, and builds and tests a simple project without manual guidance.

Authors reusable workflows, configures OIDC-based authentication to Azure or AWS without long-lived secrets, manages environment-specific variables, and implements matrix builds for multi-platform CI.
Designs multi-stage deployment pipelines with environment protection rules, self-hosted runners, composite actions, and caching strategies that materially reduce pipeline duration.

Architects the CI/CD platform across an organisation using GitHub Actions; defines reusable workflow libraries, enforces security posture via required workflows, and optimises runner cost and performance at scale.

skills.label: Azure Bicep
masteryLevels: Can read a Bicep file and explain what each resource declaration, parameter, and variable does without writing one independently.
Deploys a single Azure resource end-to-end by authoring a Bicep file with parameters, outputs, and a parameter file using the official documentation.
Authors multi-file Bicep deployments with reusable child modules, runs what-if before every production change, and integrates Bicep linting into a CI pipeline.

Designs and publishes a versioned module registry consumed across teams, implements deployment stacks for lifecycle-managed resource groups, and enforces tagging and policy compliance at the module layer.

Architects the organisation's entire IaC estate in Bicep; defines module versioning and governance strategy; evaluates new Bicep features to steer tooling decisions; mentors engineers on complex patterns including subscription and management-group scoped deployments.

resources.title: Fundamentals of Bicep — Microsoft Learn path

skills.label: Large Language Model Concepts
masteryLevels: Can explain what a large language model is, describe its input-output behaviour, and articulate common use cases without needing to understand the underlying mechanics.
Calls an LLM API with a system and user prompt, adjusts temperature and max tokens to change output style, and identifies hallucinations by cross-checking model output against known facts.

Designs effective system prompts for production use cases, applies chain-of-thought prompting to improve reasoning quality, and makes informed model selection decisions based on cost, latency, and capability tradeoffs.

Evaluates model outputs against custom benchmarks, mitigates prompt injection and hallucination at the system level, and optimises context window usage to balance quality and cost in high-volume applications.

Defines the organisation's LLM integration strategy; evaluates new model releases against production requirements; sets standards for responsible AI use, evaluation frameworks, and cost governance across engineering teams.

resources.title: Andrej Karpathy — Intro to Large Language Models (YouTube)

skills.label: Prompt Engineering
masteryLevels: Can describe the difference between zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompting and explain when each approach is most appropriate.
Writes basic system and user prompts that reliably produce structured outputs from an LLM, and iterates on failures using a test environment without manual guidance.

Designs and versions production system prompts for specific use cases, applies chain-of-thought reasoning to improve multi-step accuracy, and identifies and defends against prompt injection in user-facing inputs.

Systematically evaluates prompt performance across model versions using a test harness, implements self-consistency and meta-prompting patterns, and maintains prompt quality and safety at high request volumes.

Defines the organisation's prompt engineering standards and review cadence; publishes reusable prompt libraries consumed across teams; advises on model-specific prompting strategies as new frontier models are released.

skills.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
masteryLevels: Can explain what RAG is, why it reduces hallucinations compared to relying on model knowledge alone, and how it differs from fine-tuning as an approach.
Builds a basic RAG pipeline that chunks source documents, generates embeddings, stores them in a vector database, and retrieves relevant chunks to answer questions.

Implements hybrid search combining dense and sparse retrieval, applies re-ranking to improve retrieval precision, tunes chunk size and overlap for different document types, and measures end-to-end quality using RAGAS.

Designs production RAG systems over millions of documents, implements advanced retrieval strategies such as HyDE, multi-query retrieval, and contextual compression, and builds independent evaluation pipelines for retrieval and generation quality separately.

Architects the organisation's knowledge retrieval platform; defines embedding model selection criteria and chunking standards; evaluates retrieval architecture tradeoffs at scale; mentors engineers on domain-specific RAG design patterns.

skills.label: LLM Evaluation
masteryLevels: Can explain why LLM evaluation is necessary, describe the difference between automated and human evaluation, and name at least two frameworks used in the field.
Runs an evaluation suite against a prompt using Promptfoo or LangSmith Evals, interprets pass/fail results, and uses findings to improve prompt quality.
Designs a custom evaluation harness with task-specific assertions, integrates eval runs into a CI pipeline to catch prompt regressions before deployment, and measures RAG quality with RAGAS metrics.

Builds and curates a benchmark dataset representative of production traffic, tracks model and prompt quality across releases over time, and implements LLM-as-judge evaluation for complex qualitative outputs.

Defines the organisation's evaluation philosophy and toolchain; designs evaluation frameworks for novel task types with no existing benchmarks; presents evaluation evidence to leadership to inform model selection and deployment decisions.

skills.label: Model Selection
masteryLevels: Can name the leading frontier model families and their providers, and explain that model selection involves tradeoffs between cost, latency, capability, context window size, and data residency requirements.
Evaluates two or three candidate models on a specific task using a benchmark leaderboard and manual testing, and documents a justified selection recommendation based on measured tradeoffs.

Selects the appropriate model tier for each component of an application, benchmarks candidates against real production prompts, and accounts for pricing and context window limits when planning for scale.

Designs a model routing strategy that dispatches tasks to different models based on complexity and cost budget, evaluates fine-tuned and on-premises options, and maintains an evaluation registry tracking model performance across versions over time.

Defines the organisation's model strategy; tracks the frontier landscape and translates capability announcements into actionable engineering guidance; leads model migration projects when better options emerge; negotiates enterprise agreements with model providers.

skills.label: Token Economics
masteryLevels: Can explain what tokens are, how they determine API cost and response latency, and why token count matters when designing prompts or choosing a model.
Uses a tokenizer library to count tokens before sending requests, calculates estimated monthly API cost for a given usage pattern, and identifies the most token-expensive components of a prompt.

Implements streaming responses to improve perceived latency, uses batch inference APIs for async workloads to reduce cost, and compresses prompt templates to cut token consumption without degrading output quality.

Designs a cost attribution system tracking LLM spend per feature or user, implements prompt caching and semantic caching to eliminate redundant token usage, and models cost at scale to inform product pricing and margin decisions.

Defines the organisation's LLM cost governance framework; implements showback and chargeback for API usage across teams; sets cost-per-query SLOs for production services; advises on reserved capacity and committed-use agreements with model providers.

skills.label: Azure OpenAI Service
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure OpenAI Service is, how it differs from the OpenAI API directly, and why an enterprise would choose it for data privacy, compliance, and data residency.
Creates an Azure OpenAI resource, deploys a model, and makes a successful Chat Completions API call using the Azure SDK with endpoint and API key authentication.

Manages model deployments across dev and production environments, implements managed identity authentication to remove API keys, handles rate limit errors with exponential backoff, and selects between consumption and PTU deployments based on traffic patterns.

Architects a production Azure OpenAI setup with private endpoints, implements load balancing across multiple deployments to maximise throughput, and designs a model versioning strategy that enables controlled migration when new model versions are released.

Defines the organisation's Azure OpenAI deployment strategy and governance framework; manages PTU capacity planning and commitment decisions; advises on model retirement and migration timelines; ensures deployments satisfy data residency and compliance requirements.

resources.title: Develop AI solutions with Azure OpenAI — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure AI Foundry
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure AI Foundry is, describe how it consolidates Azure's AI development tools, and identify its key capabilities: model catalog, Prompt Flow, evaluation, and managed deployments.
Creates an Azure AI Foundry project, browses the model catalog to deploy an endpoint, and runs a basic Prompt Flow pipeline connecting an LLM to a simple data source.

Builds and tests a Prompt Flow pipeline with multiple LLM nodes and tool integrations, runs a structured evaluation against a test dataset using Foundry's built-in metrics, and deploys the validated flow as a managed online endpoint.

Designs a full development-to-production workflow in Azure AI Foundry from prompt authoring through automated evaluation to deployment, integrates Foundry pipelines with GitHub Actions for CI/CD, and configures content safety filters to meet organisational policy.

Defines the organisation's AI development lifecycle using Azure AI Foundry as the central platform; architects the hub and project structure for multi-team use; manages model catalog governance and access controls; evaluates new Foundry capabilities as the platform evolves.

resources.title: Prompt Flow in Azure AI Foundry
Get started with Azure AI Foundry — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure AI Search
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure AI Search is, how it differs from a standard keyword search engine, and identify its primary capabilities: full-text search, semantic ranking, and vector search.
Creates an Azure AI Search index, populates it with documents using an indexer or push API, and executes basic keyword search and filter queries against the index.

Configures semantic ranking to improve result relevance, implements vector search by embedding queries and documents, and builds a hybrid search pipeline combining keyword and vector scores as the retrieval layer for a RAG application.

Designs a production search architecture with AI skillsets for enriched indexing (OCR, entity extraction, key phrase), implements integrated vectorization to automate embedding generation at index time, and tunes relevance scoring for domain-specific content.

Architects the organisation's enterprise search platform on Azure AI Search; defines index schema standards, data freshness strategies, and tier selection; evaluates vector indexing options for cost and recall tradeoffs at scale; integrates AI Search as the retrieval backbone for enterprise RAG.

resources.title: Vector search in Azure AI Search
Implement knowledge mining with Azure AI Search — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure Document Intelligence
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure Document Intelligence does, describe the difference between prebuilt and custom extraction models, and identify the document types prebuilt models support out of the box.

Calls the Document Intelligence Layout API to extract text, tables, and structure from a PDF, and uses a prebuilt model such as the invoice or receipt model to extract key-value pairs from standard business documents.

Trains a custom extraction model on labelled sample documents using Document Intelligence Studio, integrates the trained model into an application pipeline, and routes low-confidence extractions to a human review queue.

Designs a document processing pipeline that classifies incoming documents by type and routes them to the appropriate prebuilt or custom model, implements post-extraction normalisation, and monitors extraction accuracy across document variations in production.

Defines the organisation's document extraction architecture; evaluates custom versus composed model strategies for complex document portfolios; advises on labelling workflows and training data volume requirements; integrates Document Intelligence as the intake layer for broader AI-powered document workflows.

resources.title: Extract data from forms with Document Intelligence — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure AI Language
masteryLevels: Can describe what Azure AI Language provides, list its prebuilt analysis capabilities (NER, sentiment, key phrase extraction, summarisation), and explain what CLU is used for in conversational applications.
Calls the Azure AI Language API to perform sentiment analysis and named entity recognition on text, interprets confidence scores, and extracts structured insights from the API response.

Configures extractive and abstractive summarisation for long documents, trains a custom text classification model for domain-specific categories in Language Studio, and integrates Language API results into an application data pipeline.

Designs a CLU model for a conversational application, iterates the model's intents and entities using Language Studio, and integrates CLU recognition with an orchestration layer to route user requests to the correct downstream service.

Defines the organisation's text analytics and conversational AI architecture using Azure AI Language; advises on when to use prebuilt capabilities versus custom models versus a general-purpose LLM; leads CLU model versioning and retraining workflows for production.

resources.title: Build natural language processing solutions — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure AI Vision
masteryLevels: Can describe what Azure AI Vision provides, identify its primary capabilities (image analysis, OCR, face detection, custom object detection), and explain the responsible AI restrictions that apply to the Face API.
Calls the Azure AI Vision Image Analysis API to extract captions, tags, and detected objects from images, and uses the Read API to perform OCR on printed and handwritten documents.

Trains a Custom Vision model for image classification or object detection on a labelled dataset in Custom Vision Studio, exports the model for API hosting or edge deployment, and integrates predictions with confidence thresholds into an application workflow.

Designs an image processing pipeline that combines Vision OCR output with Document Intelligence for structured extraction, implements Custom Vision predictions with human review fallbacks for low-confidence cases, and monitors model accuracy as production image data drifts.

Defines the organisation's computer vision architecture; evaluates Azure AI Vision against multimodal models (GPT-4o Vision) and custom CNNs for specific use cases; advises on responsible AI requirements for face recognition; leads Custom Vision model lifecycle management.

resources.title: Explore computer vision with Azure AI — MS Learn path

skills.label: Azure Machine Learning
masteryLevels: Can explain what Azure Machine Learning is, describe its relationship to Azure AI Foundry, and identify the core workspace components: compute, datasets, experiments, and model registry.
Creates an Azure ML workspace, starts a compute cluster, submits a training job from a notebook or script, and registers the resulting model artifact in the model registry.

Builds a reusable Azure ML pipeline with component steps for data preparation, training, and evaluation; uses AutoML to generate and compare candidate models for a classification task; and deploys a registered model to a managed online endpoint for real-time inference.

Designs a production MLOps workflow with automated pipeline triggers, data drift detection, and retraining pipelines; integrates Azure ML with GitHub Actions for CI/CD model promotion; and implements model monitoring to track performance against production traffic.

Defines the organisation's machine learning platform architecture; establishes workspace governance, RBAC, and compute quota management; advises on when to use Azure ML versus Azure AI Foundry for different workload types; leads MLOps maturity improvements across data science teams.

resources.title: Build and operate ML solutions with Azure — MS Learn path

skills.label: Microsoft Copilot Studio
masteryLevels: Can explain what Microsoft Copilot Studio is, describe how it differs from building a custom bot with the Bot Framework, and identify the use cases it targets — employee-facing copilots and customer service automation.
Creates a copilot in Copilot Studio with at least three topics, defines trigger phrases and conversation responses, and publishes the copilot to a Microsoft Teams channel for testing.

Designs a copilot with custom actions that call external APIs via Power Platform connectors, configures Generative Answers to ground responses in SharePoint or website content, and implements topic escalation handling for unrecognised user intents.

Builds a production copilot with OAuth-authenticated API calls, implements conversation routing across multiple topics and knowledge sources, monitors performance using Analytics dashboards, and integrates with Microsoft 365 data through Graph API actions.

Defines the organisation's copilot development standards using Copilot Studio; governs approval and publishing workflows for production copilots; evaluates Copilot Studio against custom development for enterprise scenarios; leads the Centre of Excellence for Copilot Studio adoption.

resources.title: Create bots with Microsoft Copilot Studio — MS Learn path

skills.label: Microsoft 365 Copilot Extensibility
masteryLevels: Can explain what Microsoft 365 Copilot is, describe the three extensibility options (Graph Connectors, plugins, declarative Copilot Agents), and identify when each is the appropriate approach.
Creates a basic declarative Copilot Agent using Teams Toolkit, defines its instructions and actions, and tests it within Microsoft Teams against a sample user scenario.

Builds a Graph Connector that indexes an external data source into the Microsoft 365 semantic index so Copilot can reason over it, implements a plugin that calls an external API in response to user intents, and validates agent responses against expected outcomes.

Designs a production Copilot Agent with OAuth authentication, action orchestration across multiple APIs, and access scoped to specific M365 user groups; implements adaptive card responses for rich output formatting within Teams conversations.

Defines the organisation's M365 Copilot extensibility strategy; governs which connectors and agents can be deployed and by whom; advises on data sensitivity and permissions scoping for agents with broad M365 access; tracks new extensibility capabilities as the platform evolves quarterly.

resources.title: Build plugins and connectors for Microsoft 365 Copilot — MS Learn path

skills.label: LangChain / LangGraph
masteryLevels: Can explain what LangChain is, describe the difference between a chain and an agent, and identify when LangGraph would be chosen over a simple LangChain sequence.
Builds a basic LCEL chain that retrieves documents, formats them into a prompt, and streams a response to a user following the LangChain documentation.
Designs stateful agent graphs in LangGraph with conditional routing between nodes, implements both in-context and persistent memory patterns, and integrates tool calling to handle multi-step tasks.

Architects production LangGraph applications with human-in-the-loop interruption points, optimises token usage across graph nodes, and instruments the application with LangSmith for trace-level debugging of complex graph executions.

Defines the organisation's LangChain and LangGraph architecture patterns; evaluates new framework releases and breaking changes; makes the build-versus-framework call for complex orchestration; mentors engineers on graph design tradeoffs.

skills.label: LlamaIndex
masteryLevels: Can explain what LlamaIndex does, describe how its emphasis on data indexing differs from LangChain, and identify the three core abstractions: data connectors, indexes, and query engines.
Builds a LlamaIndex application that ingests documents using a built-in data connector, creates a vector index, and answers questions over the indexed data following the quickstart guide.

Configures custom data connectors for structured and unstructured sources, selects the appropriate index type for the retrieval task, and builds a query engine with response synthesis tuned to the content domain.

Designs a LlamaIndex agentic application using function-calling patterns over multiple data sources, implements observability with LlamaTrace, and optimises the retrieval-to-response pipeline for both latency and cost.

Architects the organisation's data indexing and retrieval layer using LlamaIndex; defines connector and index design standards; evaluates LlamaIndex's workflow capabilities against production requirements; leads framework migration decisions as the API evolves.

skills.label: Semantic Kernel
masteryLevels: Can explain what Semantic Kernel is, describe its role in the Microsoft AI ecosystem alongside Azure OpenAI, and identify the three core abstractions: the kernel, plugins, and planners.
Sets up a Semantic Kernel kernel in Python or C#, registers a native function as a plugin, and invokes it from a prompt template following the Microsoft documentation.

Builds an AI application using Semantic Kernel that orchestrates multiple plugins, configures a planner to auto-select tools for a given user goal, and integrates memory for conversation history persistence.

Architects a production Semantic Kernel application with custom plugin libraries, implements function calling patterns that chain LLM reasoning with business logic, and authenticates to Azure OpenAI Service using managed identity.

Defines the organisation's Semantic Kernel integration standards; evaluates new SDK releases for breaking changes and new capabilities; designs plugin libraries that expose internal business capabilities to AI systems; leads migration planning across major versions.

resources.title: Develop AI agents with Semantic Kernel — MS Learn path

skills.label: OpenAI SDK (Python/JS)
masteryLevels: Can describe the structure of a Chat Completions API request, explain the role of system, user, and assistant message types, and identify the main OpenAI API endpoints and their purposes.

Makes authenticated API calls using the OpenAI Python or JavaScript SDK, constructs a multi-turn conversation by maintaining a message history array, and handles common API errors with basic retry logic.
Implements function calling to extend model responses with live data, streams completions to reduce perceived latency, and generates embeddings for semantic search or text classification use cases.

Builds production applications using the Assistants API with persistent threads and file attachments, implements token-efficient conversation management strategies, and routes simpler sub-tasks to cheaper models to manage cost.

Defines the organisation's OpenAI SDK usage patterns, error handling standards, and retry strategies; evaluates new API capabilities for production adoption; mentors engineers on cost optimisation and responsible API usage at scale.

skills.label: Promptfoo
masteryLevels: Can explain what Promptfoo does, describe how it differs from manual prompt testing, and identify the main output dimensions it evaluates — factual accuracy, safety, format compliance, and latency.
Installs Promptfoo, configures a provider connection to an LLM API, and runs a basic eval suite with at least two test cases and pass/fail assertions against expected outputs.

Designs a comprehensive test suite using varied assertion types (contains, similarity, LLM-as-judge, regex), configures multiple providers to compare model outputs side by side, and integrates Promptfoo runs into a CI pipeline to catch regressions on every commit.

Builds a red-team evaluation using Promptfoo's adversarial test generation, maintains a versioned test dataset that grows with discovered failure modes, and sets quality gate thresholds that block deployment when pass rates fall below defined minimums.

Defines the organisation's prompt evaluation standards using Promptfoo; designs the eval harness framework consumed across teams; advises on assertion strategy for complex qualitative outputs; uses evaluation evidence to drive model selection and prompt library decisions.

skills.label: LangSmith
masteryLevels: Can explain what LangSmith is, describe the difference between a trace and a run, and articulate why LLM application debugging without tracing is significantly harder than debugging traditional software.

Connects a LangChain or LangGraph application to LangSmith, navigates the trace viewer to follow a single run through all its steps, and identifies where an incorrect output originated in the execution chain.

Creates evaluation datasets from curated or production traces, runs automated evaluations using LangSmith's eval framework, configures monitoring dashboards for latency and error rates, and uses feedback annotation to label production runs for dataset curation.

Designs a continuous evaluation pipeline that tests prompt and model changes against curated datasets before deployment, implements custom evaluators for domain-specific quality criteria, and tracks quality trends over time to inform optimisation decisions.

Defines the organisation's LLM observability and evaluation strategy using LangSmith; sets standards for trace schema, dataset curation, and eval coverage; uses monitoring data to drive prompt engineering and model selection decisions at the engineering leadership level.

skills.label: N8N / Workflow Automation
masteryLevels: Can describe what N8N is, explain how it differs from code-first automation approaches, and identify the use cases where a visual workflow tool is more appropriate than a custom application.

Builds a simple N8N workflow triggered by a webhook that calls an LLM API, transforms the response using built-in data nodes, and writes the result to a target system such as a spreadsheet or database.

Designs multi-step N8N workflows that chain LLM calls with external APIs, implements error handling with retry logic and failure notifications, and manages credentials securely using N8N's built-in credential store.

Builds production N8N workflows with AI agent nodes, conditional branching based on LLM classification outputs, and sub-workflow composition for reusability; deploys N8N in a self-hosted configuration to satisfy data residency requirements.

Defines the organisation's workflow automation standards and tool selection criteria; architects the self-hosted N8N deployment for reliability and security; designs reusable workflow templates and shared credentials for team-wide use; evaluates N8N against other platforms for enterprise suitability.

skills.label: Vector Databases
masteryLevels: Can explain what a vector embedding is, why vectors enable semantic similarity search, and describe the core purpose of a vector database — storing and querying high-dimensional embeddings by distance.

Creates a vector collection in a managed service such as Pinecone or a local Chroma instance, inserts embedded documents, and queries for semantically similar results using cosine or dot-product distance.

Selects the appropriate vector database for a use case based on scalability, cost, and hosting requirements; configures metadata filtering to combine vector similarity with structured attribute constraints; integrates the database into a production RAG pipeline.

Designs a vector database architecture with index partitioning for query performance, implements incremental embedding update strategies to avoid full re-indexing, and benchmarks ANN algorithm options against recall and latency requirements for the specific use case.

Defines the organisation's vector storage strategy; evaluates managed versus self-hosted options against cost, compliance, and operational overhead; architects multi-tenancy patterns for shared vector infrastructure; advises teams on embedding model selection and index design tradeoffs.

resources.title: pgvector — open-source vector extension for Postgres

skills.label: Agent Architecture Design
masteryLevels: Can explain what an AI agent is, describe the basic ReAct (Reason + Act) loop, and articulate how agents differ from single-shot LLM calls.
Builds a simple single-agent system using a framework such as LangGraph that reasons over a set of tools and produces a final answer, following documentation.
Designs agents with well-defined tool sets, implements both short-term working memory and long-term memory retrieval, and chooses between ReAct and Plan-and-Execute patterns based on task complexity.

Architects multi-step stateful agents with robust error recovery, interruption handling, and human-in-the-loop checkpoints; evaluates execution traces to diagnose failure modes and optimise token usage per run.

Defines the organisation's agent architecture standards; evaluates emerging agentic frameworks against production reliability and cost requirements; designs agent systems that are safe, auditable, and cost-predictable at scale.

resources.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in LLMs (arXiv)

skills.label: Multi-Agent Orchestration
masteryLevels: Can describe why multi-agent systems are used, explain the difference between sequential and parallel agent orchestration, and name at least two multi-agent frameworks.
Wires together two specialised agents in a simple pipeline using LangGraph or AutoGen, passing outputs from one agent as inputs to the next following documentation.

Designs workflows with a supervisor agent that routes tasks to specialised sub-agents, implements handoff protocols, and handles partial failures so the pipeline recovers without restarting from scratch.

Architects parallel multi-agent systems with dynamic task decomposition, implements human-in-the-loop intervention points, and evaluates traces across all agents to diagnose coordination failures and reduce cost per completed task.

Defines the organisation's multi-agent architecture patterns and guardrails; evaluates competing orchestration frameworks against production reliability and cost requirements; leads the design of complex long-running agentic workflows used across business domains.

skills.label: Tool / Function Calling
masteryLevels: Can explain what function calling is, describe how a model decides when to invoke a tool versus answer directly, and identify the JSON schema format used to define tool signatures.
Defines a simple tool schema, registers it with a model API, handles the tool call response to execute the function, and returns results back to the model to complete the response.

Designs a well-structured tool library with precise descriptions that guide reliable tool selection, implements input validation and graceful error handling so agents recover cleanly from failed tool calls.

Architects a tool registry with versioning and access controls, implements parallel tool calling for independent sub-tasks, and evaluates tool selection accuracy across agent traces to identify and fix description ambiguities.

Defines the organisation's tool design standards and naming conventions; builds shared tool libraries consumed across multiple agents and teams; advises on tool granularity tradeoffs to minimise agent loops and hallucinated parameters.

skills.label: Agent Observability
masteryLevels: Can explain why observing agent execution is harder than observing traditional software, and describe what needs to be captured — steps, tools called, tokens consumed, and decisions made.
Configures LangSmith or a similar tracing tool to capture agent runs, navigates trace visualisations to follow a single agent execution step by step, and identifies where an agent went wrong.

Instruments an agent system with structured step-level logging, calculates cost per run from token usage across all steps, sets up alerts for runs exceeding latency or cost thresholds, and uses traces to diagnose systematic failure patterns.

Designs an observability platform for a fleet of agents that aggregates traces, surfaces anomalies, and tracks quality metrics over time; implements evaluation runs against production traces to catch regressions after prompt or model changes.

Defines the organisation's agent observability standards including trace schema, retention policy, and alerting thresholds; builds tooling that makes agent behaviour auditable for compliance; presents agent reliability metrics to executive stakeholders.

skills.label: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
masteryLevels: Can explain what the Model Context Protocol is, why Anthropic introduced it, and how it standardises the way AI applications connect to external tools and data sources.
Runs an existing MCP server locally, connects it to an MCP-compatible client such as Claude Desktop or Cursor, and inspects the tools and resources it exposes.
Builds a custom MCP server that exposes domain-specific tools and resources, integrates it with an LLM application, and validates that the model correctly selects and invokes the exposed tools.

Designs a production-grade MCP server with authentication, input validation, and error handling; implements multiple capability types (tools, prompts, resources); and deploys it as a shared service consumed by multiple AI applications.

Defines the organisation's MCP server design standards; evaluates the MCP ecosystem for servers to adopt; architects a composable tool layer that exposes the organisation's capabilities to AI systems in a governed, reusable way.

resources.title: Anthropic — Introducing the Model Context Protocol

skills.label: AI Security
masteryLevels: Can explain the difference between direct and indirect prompt injection, describe why AI systems introduce attack surfaces not present in traditional software, and name the primary threat categories in the OWASP LLM Top 10.

Applies basic input sanitisation to user-supplied prompts, implements output filtering to catch sensitive data in model responses, and tests an AI endpoint manually using common prompt injection payloads.

Designs a multi-layer defence for a production AI application covering input validation, system prompt hardening, output scanning for PII and policy violations, and rate limiting to constrain exfiltration attempts.

Architects an AI security posture across the full attack surface — model, data pipeline, tool integrations, and API gateway — integrates automated adversarial testing into CI, and defines incident response procedures specific to AI system compromise.

Defines the organisation's AI threat model and security framework; leads red-team exercises against production AI systems; advises on the security controls required for enterprise compliance; represents AI security risk in architecture review boards.

resources.title: Anthropic — Responsible scaling policy and safety

skills.label: Responsible AI
masteryLevels: Can explain the core responsible AI principles (fairness, accountability, transparency, reliability, privacy, inclusiveness) and describe one real-world case where an AI system caused measurable harm.

Identifies potential bias sources in a dataset or prompt design for a specific use case, documents system assumptions and limitations in a feature spec, and proposes a human review step proportionate to the decision stakes.

Conducts an AI impact assessment for a new feature, designs human-in-the-loop checkpoints at high-stakes decision points, and produces a model card that communicates system capabilities, limitations, and intended use to downstream stakeholders.

Designs fairness evaluation pipelines that measure model performance across demographic subgroups, implements explainability tooling so affected users can understand decisions, and defines escalation and appeal paths when automated decisions are challenged.

Leads the organisation's responsible AI programme; defines the governance framework, approval gates, and accountability structure for AI products; advises on the EU AI Act and applicable regulations; represents responsible AI risk in product and engineering leadership.

resources.title: EU AI Act — European Parliament overview
Google Model Cards — about and examples

skills.label: Content Safety
masteryLevels: Can describe the main harm categories AI content safety systems address — hate speech, violence, sexual content, self-harm — and explain why keyword filtering alone is insufficient for LLM outputs.
Integrates Azure Content Safety API into an AI application to classify input and output text, reviews flagged results, and configures severity thresholds appropriate to the application's risk level.
Designs a layered content safety strategy combining Azure Content Safety, model-level system prompt instructions, and custom blocklists tailored to the application's domain and user base.

Builds a content safety evaluation pipeline that tests model outputs against a curated harm dataset, tracks safety metric trends across model updates, and tunes threshold configurations to balance false positives against harm recall.

Defines the organisation's content safety architecture and harm taxonomy; advises product teams on risk-proportionate controls; leads safety incident reviews when harmful content reaches production; owns content safety requirements in model selection decisions.

skills.label: AI Governance
masteryLevels: Can explain why AI governance differs from traditional IT governance and describe the key questions any governed AI system must answer: who owns it, what data does it use, what decisions does it make, and how is it monitored.
Creates an inventory of AI services in use within a project, documents model versions and deployment dates, and configures access controls so only authorised identities can call production AI APIs.

Implements audit logging for all AI API calls capturing inputs, outputs, model version, and latency; sets up cost guardrails with budget alerts; and maintains a model registry tracking which version is deployed to each environment.

Designs an AI governance framework covering the full model lifecycle — risk classification and approval before deployment, monitoring in production, incident response, and retirement — and enforces it via policy guardrails in CI/CD pipelines.

Defines the organisation's AI governance policy, risk tiers, and review committee structure; ensures deployments satisfy regulatory requirements including the EU AI Act; leads responses to AI audit findings; reports AI risk posture to leadership and the board.

resources.title: Azure AI services — responsible use overview
EU AI Act — European Commission regulatory framework

skills.label: Data Privacy in AI
masteryLevels: Can identify categories of PII and protected health information, explain why sending personal data to third-party LLM APIs creates privacy risk, and describe data residency as a concept.
Reviews an AI feature design to identify personal data flows, documents what PII reaches the model, and applies basic tokenisation or field masking to remove sensitive values before API calls.

Implements PII detection and redaction in an AI data pipeline using a library such as Microsoft Presidio, configures Azure OpenAI Service with private endpoints to satisfy data residency requirements, and documents data handling in a privacy impact assessment.

Designs a privacy-preserving AI architecture that prevents personal data from leaving defined geographic boundaries, applies anonymisation or synthetic data techniques for evaluation datasets, and ensures the AI system satisfies GDPR data subject rights including the right to erasure.

Defines the organisation's data privacy framework for AI; owns data processing agreements with AI providers; leads privacy impact assessments for high-risk systems; advises legal and compliance on novel AI privacy risks; represents data privacy in AI product governance.

resources.title: Microsoft Presidio — PII detection and anonymisation
ICO guidance on AI and data protection

skills.label: AI System Testing
masteryLevels: Can explain why AI system testing differs from traditional software testing — non-determinism, semantic correctness, emergent failures — and describe what red-teaming means in an AI context.
Writes manual adversarial test cases targeting a specific AI feature, covering boundary inputs, unexpected user intents, and harmful prompts, and documents the failure patterns found for remediation.

Designs and runs a structured red-team exercise against a production AI feature, builds a regression eval suite that runs in CI to catch quality or safety regressions across model updates, and uses A/B testing to measure the impact of prompt changes on user outcomes.

Builds an automated adversarial testing pipeline that generates diverse attack scenarios, maintains a curated golden dataset for regression testing, and applies statistical significance testing when comparing model versions to prevent premature A/B conclusions.

Defines the organisation's AI testing strategy across functional, safety, and adversarial dimensions; establishes the red-team programme governance and cadence; advises on what level of testing evidence satisfies compliance and deployment approval requirements.

skills.label: MLOps Concepts
masteryLevels: Can explain what MLOps is, describe how it applies DevOps principles to machine learning, and identify the key challenges it addresses — reproducibility, monitoring, and controlled retraining.

Versions a trained model using an experiment tracking tool such as MLflow, documents the training dataset and hyperparameters as metadata alongside the artifact, and registers the model in a central model registry.

Detects model drift by comparing production prediction distributions against a baseline, triggers a retraining pipeline when drift exceeds a threshold, and uses a feature store to ensure training-serving feature consistency.

Designs a full MLOps lifecycle with automated data lineage tracking, multi-environment model promotion gates, and A/B deployment infrastructure; implements data quality checks that fail the pipeline before wasted training compute.

Defines the organisation's MLOps maturity model and roadmap; establishes platform-level tooling standards for experiment tracking, model registry, and monitoring; leads MLOps culture adoption across data science teams.

resources.title: Google Cloud — MLOps: continuous delivery and automation pipelines

skills.label: AI/ML Pipelines
masteryLevels: Can explain what an ML pipeline is, describe how it differs from a standard data pipeline, and identify the main stages in a typical ML pipeline: data ingestion, preprocessing, training, evaluation, and model registration.

Converts a notebook-based training script into a parameterised, reusable pipeline using Azure ML Pipelines or MLflow, runs it on a compute cluster, and compares experiment results using the tracking UI.

Builds a multi-step Azure ML pipeline with reusable component definitions, caches expensive preprocessing steps to reduce cost, and integrates the pipeline into a CI/CD workflow so model updates trigger automatically on data or code changes.

Designs a production ML pipeline platform with component versioning, parallel hyperparameter tuning, and automated evaluation gates that block model promotion when performance falls below baseline; implements cross-framework portability using MLflow for tracking.

Defines the organisation's ML pipeline architecture standards; evaluates pipeline orchestration options (Azure ML, Kubeflow, Vertex AI Pipelines) against team skills and platform requirements; leads adoption of shared reusable component libraries across data science teams.

skills.label: Model Deployment Patterns
masteryLevels: Can describe the main deployment patterns — online endpoints, batch inference, shadow mode, canary releases, and A/B deployment — and explain when each is appropriate given latency, throughput, and risk requirements.
Deploys a trained model to an Azure ML managed online endpoint, sends test requests to verify predictions, and monitors basic endpoint latency and throughput metrics.

Implements a batch inference pipeline for high-volume asynchronous scoring, configures a traffic split between two model versions for A/B testing, and uses shadow mode to evaluate a new model against live traffic without exposing its predictions to users.

Designs a zero-downtime deployment strategy with canary releases and automated rollback triggers, implements request-level input/output logging for drift detection, and integrates model deployment into a CI/CD pipeline with performance-gated promotion.

Defines the organisation's model deployment standards including rollout patterns, rollback procedures, and SLO definitions; advises on infrastructure choices (managed endpoints vs. containers vs. serverless) for different model and traffic types; leads incident response when deployed models underperform.

resources.title: Safe rollout for online endpoints — Azure ML

skills.label: Embedding & Vector Management
masteryLevels: Can explain what an embedding model does, describe how model size, dimensionality, and domain training affect retrieval quality, and identify the key decisions in embedding pipeline design: model choice, chunking, and storage.

Selects an embedding model appropriate to the use case, chunks source documents into passages, generates embeddings via API or a local model, and stores them in a vector database with associated metadata.

Evaluates embedding model quality using retrieval benchmarks such as MTEB scores, implements a chunking strategy that balances context completeness with retrieval precision, and designs an incremental update pipeline that re-embeds only changed documents rather than the full corpus.

Manages an embedding model migration — re-embedding a large corpus when upgrading to a higher-quality model — with zero downtime by maintaining two indexes in parallel during the transition; implements embedding caching to reduce repeated API cost for frequently queried content.

Defines the organisation's embedding model governance strategy; tracks the embedding model landscape and evaluates when migration provides sufficient quality lift to justify re-indexing cost; designs the production embedding pipeline for reliability, cost efficiency, and model upgrade resilience.

resources.title: MTEB leaderboard — Massive Text Embedding Benchmark

skills.label: LLMOps
masteryLevels: Can explain what LLMOps is, describe how it differs from traditional MLOps — non-deterministic outputs, prompt as code, no gradient-based retraining — and identify the operational concerns specific to LLM-based applications in production.
Versions prompt templates alongside application code in source control, logs LLM API cost and latency per endpoint, and sets up alerts when cost or latency exceeds defined thresholds.

Implements a prompt change management process where every update is evaluated against a test dataset before deployment, defines latency SLOs for LLM endpoints, and designs a feedback loop that captures user signals to curate examples for future evaluation datasets.

Architects a model swap strategy that allows switching the underlying LLM without application code changes, implements A/B testing infrastructure for comparing prompt and model versions against business metrics, and builds cost attribution dashboards showing LLM spend by feature and team.

Defines the organisation's LLMOps operating model; establishes the governance process for model changes, prompt updates, and evaluation gate thresholds; designs the feedback-to-evaluation pipeline connecting production signals back to improvement cycles; leads LLMOps platform adoption across AI engineering teams.

skills.label: Python for AI
masteryLevels: Can write basic Python scripts that use pandas to load and inspect a dataset, install AI SDK packages, and make a synchronous API call to an LLM using a standard library wrapper.
Structures a Python AI project with a virtual environment, uses pandas and numpy to prepare and transform data before passing it to a model, and runs exploratory experiments in Jupyter notebooks.

Implements async API calls to LLM endpoints using asyncio to handle concurrent requests without blocking, wraps LLM and vector database clients in reusable abstractions, and serves an AI feature via a FastAPI endpoint with input validation and error handling.

Designs a production Python AI service with async request handling, structured logging, retry logic for transient API failures, and performance profiling to identify inference pipeline bottlenecks; packages the application for containerised deployment.

Defines the organisation's Python AI development standards covering project structure, dependency management, async patterns, and testing approach; evaluates new AI libraries and SDK releases for production adoption; mentors engineers on building reliable, observable Python AI services.

skills.label: TypeScript / JavaScript for AI
masteryLevels: Can explain why TypeScript is commonly used for AI web applications, identify the major JS/TS SDKs for LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Azure), and describe what the Vercel AI SDK provides.
Makes authenticated API calls to an LLM using the OpenAI or Anthropic TypeScript SDK in a Node.js application, handles the async response correctly, and logs model output.
Implements streaming LLM responses in a Node.js backend and renders them progressively in a React component using the Vercel AI SDK useChat hook, reducing perceived latency for end users.

Builds a production AI-powered web application with server-side streaming, error boundaries for model failures, optimistic UI updates, and type-safe tool call schemas shared between client and server using Zod.

Defines the organisation's TypeScript AI application architecture; evaluates new AI SDKs and React server patterns for production adoption; establishes streaming and type-safety standards for LLM-powered features; mentors frontend engineers on integrating AI capabilities into existing applications.

skills.label: API Integration Patterns
masteryLevels: Can describe the common patterns for integrating LLM APIs into systems — synchronous, asynchronous, event-driven, and queue-based — and explain the tradeoffs in terms of latency, reliability, and cost.
Implements a webhook endpoint that receives an external event, triggers an LLM API call, and writes the result to a database with basic success and error handling.

Designs an event-driven AI integration where incoming data triggers model inference via a message queue, implements background processing to handle long-running jobs without blocking the API response, and adds dead-letter queue handling for failed inference tasks.

Architects a queue-based inference platform that fully decouples ingestion from processing, implements back-pressure to prevent queue overflows during traffic spikes, and designs idempotent inference handlers that safely retry without producing duplicate outputs.

Defines the organisation's AI integration architecture patterns; establishes standards for async job handling, retry policies, and observability; evaluates messaging infrastructure (Azure Service Bus, Event Hubs, AWS SQS) against AI workload requirements; mentors engineers on reliable AI-powered event-driven system design.

resources.title: Patterns of Distributed Systems — Martin Fowler

skills.label: Document Processing
masteryLevels: Can describe the main approaches to extracting text from documents — direct parsing, OCR, and AI-powered extraction — and explain when each is appropriate based on document type and quality.
Extracts text and structure from PDFs using a library such as PyMuPDF or pdfplumber, parses HTML using BeautifulSoup, and normalises extracted text into a consistent format for downstream processing.

Builds a document processing pipeline that handles multiple file types (PDF, Word, HTML, image-heavy scans), applies OCR via Azure Document Intelligence for scanned content, and enriches extracted text with metadata (source, date, document type) before indexing.

Designs a production document ingestion pipeline with file type detection, format-specific extraction handlers, error handling for malformed documents, and chunking strategies tuned to the downstream retrieval task — balancing context completeness against index cost.

Defines the organisation's document processing architecture; evaluates extraction tools and AI models (traditional OCR vs. multimodal LLMs) against accuracy and cost for the document portfolio; leads design of the document intake layer for enterprise knowledge bases and RAG systems.

skills.label: Search & Knowledge Architecture
masteryLevels: Can explain the difference between keyword, semantic, and hybrid search, describe what a knowledge graph is, and identify why structured knowledge representation matters for AI applications that need precise, reliable retrieval.

Implements a basic hybrid search combining BM25 keyword scoring with vector similarity in a search index, and explains how the two score types are merged using Reciprocal Rank Fusion or a weighted sum.

Designs a hybrid search architecture as the retrieval backbone of a RAG system, defines a content taxonomy that organises documents into categories enabling metadata-filtered retrieval, and implements metadata enrichment at ingestion time to improve search precision.

Architects a knowledge architecture that combines vector search with graph relationships, enabling the system to answer questions requiring entity traversal rather than just passage retrieval; designs the ontology mapping domain concepts to indexed entities and relationships.

Defines the organisation's enterprise knowledge architecture strategy; evaluates when a knowledge graph justifies its added complexity over vector search alone; leads taxonomy and ontology design with domain experts; architects the shared knowledge layer underpinning multiple AI-powered products.

resources.title: Azure AI Search — hybrid search overview
Azure AI Search — hybrid ranking with RRF
